Getting Started with Async/Await in TypeScript

Async/await is the modern way to handle asynchronous operations in TypeScript. It makes asynchronous code look and behave like synchronous code, dramatically improving readability and making error handling straightforward. This tutorial covers the essential patterns every TypeScript developer should know.

Basic Usage

The async keyword marks a function as asynchronous, and await pauses execution until a promise resolves:

async function fetchUser(id: string): Promise<User> {
  const response = await fetch(`/api/users/${id}`);
  if (!response.ok) {
    throw new Error(`Failed to fetch user: ${response.status}`);
  }
  return response.json();
}

Error Handling

Use standard try/catch blocks to handle errors in async functions. This is far more readable than chaining .catch() handlers on promises:

async function loadData(): Promise<void> {
  try {
    const user = await fetchUser('123');
    const posts = await fetchPosts(user.id);
    console.log(`Loaded ${posts.length} posts for ${user.name}`);
  } catch (error) {
    console.error('Failed to load data:', error);
  }
}

Parallel Execution

When you need to run multiple async operations concurrently, use Promise.all:

async function loadDashboard(userId: string) {
  const [user, posts, notifications] = await Promise.all([
    fetchUser(userId),
    fetchPosts(userId),
    fetchNotifications(userId),
  ]);

  return { user, posts, notifications };
}

This approach runs all three fetch operations simultaneously rather than sequentially, significantly reducing total wait time. It is one of the most important performance patterns for async TypeScript code.
